data.title: CDZ:CA: This Idea Doesn't Fit In The Market
data.description:
What is CDZ:CA?

The iShares S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index ETF (CDZ:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of ~$1Bn CAD that invests in stocks designed to provide exposure to a portfolio of Canadian dividend-paying stocks. BlackRock, and most of the other big Canadian banks/fund managers, have multiple ETF offerings that end up providing various investment portfolios that track very similar metrics. While CDZ:CA is somewhat liquid and has an easily verifiable portfolio and mandate, this article seeks to compare this offering with some other passively managed offerings of Canadian equity portfolios because they are often so similar and not worth the high fees.

CDZ:CA's benchmark target is the S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Total Return Index (CAD), and after further review, I believe that CDZ:CA is not worth the investment. The fund is undifferentiated from other passively managed equity funds in Canada and charges higher fees than other ETF peers, like iShares S&P/TSX 60 Index ETF (XIU:CA), another passive TSX 60 ETF with a similar profile, or even XDV:CA, which I just wrote about recently, noting that it's another undifferentiated ETF with higher fees. If investors want to get exposure to higher dividend-paying Canadian stocks, they can easily buy these other ETFs or recreate the portfolio CDZ:CA has, since the portfolio is mostly weighted in about 10 easily identifiable large-cap names on the TSX.

Fund Breakdown

CDZ:CA was launched on September 8, 2006, by BlackRock with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that replicates exposure to the largest dividend growers in the Canadian market. The S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index was created by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and consists of stocks issued by Canadian issuers who have increased dividends in at least four of the past five years. A company can maintain its dividend rate for a max of two consecutive years over a five-year time frame and still remain eligible. While in theory, the ETF sounds like a good way to lower equity risk with dividend aristocrats, the reality is that the fund has been a dud. The Canadian market is already very mature, with the large-cap index paying an average yield of about 2.5% anyway.

BlackRock is trying to sell investors on a dividend strategy, but in Canada, it doesn't really make sense – and history has shown it to be an underperformer. Below is the returns chart comparing CDZ:CA to XIU:CA over the past 3 months as the market has ripped higher over the summer. XIU:CA, the TSX 60 passive ETF with similar characteristics, has significantly outperformed CDZ:CA.

Because the largest dividend payers tend to be the banks and telecoms, all these funds have similar results anyway because Canada is simply not that big of a market. You don't need dividend strategies because the few big players within banks, insurance, resources, telecom, and real estate all pay ~2-6% dividends.

Additionally, CDZ:CA has an expense ratio of 0.66%, much higher than XIU:CA at 0.18% and XDV:CA at 0.55%. The only rationale for buying into the fund is if the investor is interested in the corporate class as a whole. CDZ:CA is a corporate class fund, and corporate class funds are essentially mutual funds structured as different share classes under a single corporation. That means that there are over a dozen funds in a pool under one entity (i.e., CDZ:CA is the "dividend" focused fund in the class, and there would be other equity and bond funds). The key advantage is in tax efficiency, particularly for Canadian investors in non-registered accounts, because investors can switch between different funds within the same corporate class structure without triggering capital gains. Distributions are often paid out as capital gains or Canadian dividends, both of which are taxed more favorably than interest income. The downside, though, is that corporate class funds typically come with higher management fees, less transparency, and tend to underperform other liquid ETFs. Sure, you can save some money on taxes, but if the fund is underperforming anyway... what's the point? Meanwhile, non-class ETFs are easier to trade, are more cost-efficient, and available in a wide range of sectors and strategies.

Another reason to avoid chasing this fund strategy is due to the index itself. The S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index is designed to measure the performance of companies included in Canada that have followed a policy of stable or increased dividends every year for at least five years. However, upon a deep dive into the benchmark, a stock can maintain their dividend for up to two years within that period... so the stocks don't really need to be dividend aristocrats, do they?

Performance aside, I think this fund is charging too high a fee for such a simple portfolio calculation. In terms of liquidity, CDZ:CA trades about 12,500 shares per day worth ~$500K; not ideal for whales. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. In conclusion, the theory behind the fund isn't bad, but it's unnecessary given the stock underperformance (even accounting for the marginally higher yield vs. passive Canadian equity index peers) and the bevy of high dividend-paying stocks in Canada.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing CDZ:CA's portfolio, there are minor differences in the portfolio breakdown to the main indexes. As of Sep. 16, 2025, the portfolio resembles XIU:CA, with almost identical sector exposures, other than a slightly lower weighting in financials, with the difference in REITs. The bottom line is... is this fund worth paying 3-6x the fees for underperformance, marginally lower beta, and a mediocre Sharpe ratio? I don't think so.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.84 means that the fund will have slightly less volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results and makes sense given dividend payers are generally more mature companies. The ETF has a Sharpe ratio of 0.79, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, and unfortunately 0.79 isn't that good - or at least not good enough to justify triple the fees while other passive ETFs have outperformed in Canada.

Another risk worth mentioning is the tariff uncertainty, which is somewhat mitigated with the USMCA trade deal remaining intact. While companies have eaten a good chunk of the tariffs over the past few months, no matter how you slice it, corporate earnings or higher inflation will emerge, and extended tariffs should lead to a material dip in the market, prompting potential dividend cuts along with stock price drops. While the Canadian market has held up for now, it's worth monitoring. With rates in Canada already low, it's tough to see a clear path forward without some pain, given our intertwined economies with the U.S.

Bottom Line

Ultimately, the bottom line is that this fund is not really worth an investment. The fund has a higher expense ratio and has underperformed peers, doesn't have a ton of liquidity, and has minimal differentiation. The Canadian market has many large-cap dividend payers - stick to buying the main index ETFs to ensure balanced outperformance in Canadian equity... and if you really like dividend stocks, you can easily buy XIU:CA and add a BCE or ENB on your own... even though they are already in the broad market ETFs anyway.

data.title: Key Factoids Point To How Overvalued The Nasdaq Has Become
data.description:
Despite a faltering jobs market, rising auto and student loan defaults, concerning CRE delinquencies in the Office and Multifamily spaces, and a collapsing housing sector in many regions of the country like Austin, Las Vegas, Oakland, Denver and most of Florida, the NASDAQ is trading at all-time highs.

This huge rally has been driven by enthusiasm for all things AI-related and the performance of the Magnificent Seven. This has resulted in the overall market trading at extreme levels using many traditional valuation metrics. The tech-heavy NASDAQ appears particularly vulnerable to a significant pullback at some point on the short-term horizon. In today's article, I will highlight numerous factoids in four key categories that point to how overbought the NASDAQ has become after advancing some 125% since the beginning of 2023.

Market Capitalization:

NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has a market capitalization of nearly $4.3 trillion. That is higher than the annual GDP of the U.K. ($3.6 trillion) and France ($3.2 trillion). It is getting close to the annual GDP of Germany ($4.7 trillion), the largest economy in Europe. Microsoft ($3.8 trillion) also has a larger market cap than the GDP of France and the U.K., and Alphabet ($3.05 trillion) is close.

To put this in perspective, at the end of 1999, Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) reached a market cap of $600 billion and was the most valued stock in the market. The U.K.'s GDP in 1999 was just under £1.7 trillion. France's GDP that year was just under €1.5 trillion.

Bifurcation:


This has led to something investors have not experienced since the Internet Boom. The top 10 stocks by market capitalization currently account for approximately 40% of the U.S. equity market. The only thing that has changed since 1999 is the top ten names with the exception of Microsoft. The market soon crashed the last time the market became this bifurcated. Will this time be different?

Demands of AI:

One of the reasons the markets are so bifurcated is that the costs and demands of the AI revolution are completely different from those of the last major technology paradigm, the birth of the internet in the late 20th century. Networking equipment, web programmers and servers were relatively expensive back in those days, but those costs dropped dramatically over time. However, the costs of deploying internet solutions were not prohibitive, and hundreds of thousands of businesses went online fairly quickly.

Massive amounts of capital were raised and companies IPO'd based on their potential as well as 'eyeballs' and not profitability. This eventually led to many of these companies, like Webvan and Pets.com going bust. This is not the case here in the AI revolution. Only the most established and largest firms like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) can afford the billions of dollars to build an AI data center.

In addition, deployment of the internet did not have huge demands on the electrical grid and fresh water supplies. These huge impacts are resulting in more and more communities like Tucson and many cities in Virginia pushing back on permitting these huge data centers. If this resistance grows it could slow tech spending growth, which could be quite problematic given how dependent GDP growth has become on this economic engine.

Traditional Valuation Metrics:

We end this piece by looking at a couple of traditional valuation metrics around the NASDAQ. Now the S&P 500 on a price to sales ratio is selling right near 3.2 times revenues. The only time the index has been valued higher was right at the end of the Internet Boom.

However, that is downright cheap compared to what the NASDAQ is trading at using that metric as the tech-heavy index currently trades at nearly double that at 6.35 times sales. The NASDAQ also trades right at 34 times trailing 12-month earnings (non-GAAP).

Given the factoids above, I believe the chances of at least a correction (10% decline) over the next six months are quite high. Therefore, my portfolio is currently positioned quite conservatively. Approximately one quarter is in short-term treasuries. Roughly one percent is in what I call Armageddon trades. These are long-dated, deep out-of-the-money bear put spreads against the S&P 500 and NASDAQ. These will pay off between 12 and 20 times if the S&P 500 or NASDAQ falls by 20% to 30% over the next 12-15 months. I consider that cheap portfolio insurance.

The rest of my holdings are within covered call positions on the few stocks I am finding in the market that still sport reasonable valuations. A similar portfolio configuration for most of 2024 led to a gain of right at 17% for my portfolio last year. Slightly less than the S&P 500 returned in 2024, but with much less risk. The portfolio is tracking towards similar returns to this point in 2025.

data.title: Wendy's: The Makings Of A Value Trap
data.description:
Wendy's (NASDAQ:WEN), one of the largest quick-service chains in the United States, has been quite a poor investment, with shares essentially flat over 10 years, and down 55% over the past five.

At 10 times earnings, there's a set of investors that might be looking at Wendy's for a value play, but I argue it has all the makings of a value trap.

Let's dive in.

Introduction To Wendy's Underperformance Decade

Wendy's was quite the success story in its early years. With a special focus on made-to-order food and a unique franchising model on a per-geography basis, Wendy's expansion was rapid.

Today, Wendy's has over 7,300 restaurants in 35 countries, the majority of which are franchised and located in the United States.

While Wendy's continues to grow its footprint and expand internationally, its growth and margins have been inconsistent, which I attribute to a lack of differentiation, relatively weaker brand value, and intense competition.

Indeed, Wendy's has been a huge laggard compared to other mature chain operators like McDonald's (MCD), Yum Brands (YUM), or Restaurant Brands (QSR). It has also been lagging far behind the market in terms of total return.

To sum it up, Wendy's investors over the past decade are likely very disappointed with their results. The question is, what caused it, and could it change going forward?

Wendy's Financials Give An Easy Explanation

Over the past 10 years, Wendy's revenues grew at a miserable 1.5% CAGR, which is less than inflation. EBITDA grew at 3.7% annually, while net and operating income grew about 5%.

Last quarter was no better, with global sales declining 1.8%, driven by a 2.9% decline in same-restaurant sales. Restaurant margins dropped 30 bps, and adjusted EBITDA rose by 2.5%. In short, a quarter that's very similar to the last decade's trend.

With those weak numbers in mind, this would be a good spot to go over what makes a good restaurant investment.

Investing In Restaurants - Framework

Several legendary investors had great success with restaurants. Two famous examples are Peter Lynch and his Dunkin' Donuts investment, and Bill Ackman's Chipotle (CMG) investment.

These stories are only one reason why investors are drawn to investing in the sector. Another reason is that restaurant stocks give a sense of confidence because of the possibility of getting first-hand experience. Also, above all, they can simply be excellent businesses.

Once a chain settles on a winning menu and strong unit economics, there aren't too many limitations for expansion, and all they have to do is duplicate the already-proven formula over and over.

When analyzing a restaurant business, investors should analyze the company through a simple framework. Are unit economics attractive? Is there runway for unit expansion? Is the management team high-quality?

Under store economics, investors should look at same-restaurant growth, average unit volumes, and restaurant-level margins.

To assess expansion runway, investors should compare the chain's existing store count against its closest competitors and analyze the company's geographic footprint.

For management quality, it's key to focus on the company's balance sheet, management's ability to deliver better results compared to peers, and whether they find innovative ways to drive differentiation.

Let's analyze Wendy's through that framework.

Weak Unit Economics, Saturated Footprint, High Debt

Looking at Wendy's underwhelming headline numbers, it shouldn't be shocking to anyone that a deeper look into the company also surfaces issues.

Unit Economics

Wendy's has been a constant member of the bottom group when it comes to same-restaurant sales figures. It placed at the 15th spot over the past 10 quarters on average, trailing close peers including McDonald's, Burger King, and KFC.

Meanwhile, Wendy's average unit volumes of $2 million are below average, and its restaurant-level margins of 16.2% are also mediocre.

Footprint

When it comes to expansion runway, it seems Wendy's is pretty close to its closest peers, and given the relatively weak positioning on unit economics, it's difficult to support an aggressive unit opening outlook in the US (broadly speaking, more units hurt unit economics due to cannibalization).

Management Quality

Then, there's debt. Wendy's is more leveraged than most of its peers, certainly ones that have no net debt at all, like Chipotle, CAVA (CAVA), or Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), but also compared to peers who do use debt.

It's also lagging the competition when it comes to brand awareness and making noise through marketing and promotions.

Lastly, we saw under unit economics that Wendy's hasn't shown an ability to outperform peers; in fact, the opposite is true.

Valuation & Value Traps

I think it's fair to say we've established Wendy's as a laggard and found an explanation for the stock's weak performance over the past decade.

All that raises a simple question: Why are investors still considering an investment in Wendy's? Well, the answer is presumably valuation.

At $9.50 a share, Wendy's trades at 10 times forward earnings, which places it far below the peers we've discussed.

Investors tell themselves that at such a low valuation, all the issues are already priced in, and that a potential turnaround could provide significant upside.

To me, that's a perfect example of a value trap, as there's no tangible sign of growth acceleration, and there's nothing to lean on for an improvement in Wendy's competitive positioning or unit economics.

Conclusion

The easiest way to underperform, in my view, is to invest in a company just because of its low valuation, when fundamentals are objectively weak.

The way I see it, it's very likely that Wendy's stock will continue to underperform its peers, despite a much lower valuation.

At the end of the day, Wendy's lags on unit economics, expansion potential, and qualitative aspects, which are the pieces that make a good restaurant investment.

As such, I rate Wendy's at a "Hold," though I encourage investors to exit their positions and allocate their money elsewhere.

data.title: FactSet: I've Been Waiting Several Years For This Moment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
After years of waiting, it's time to invest

The first time that I discovered FactSet Research Systems Inc. (NYSE:FDS), I was immediately impressed by its solidity. More than 40 years of constant revenue growth, a dividend aristocrat, and a stable profit margin of 23%-24%. As if this is not enough, the debt is negligible, and customer satisfaction is one of FactSet’s strengths. In fact, the retention rate is over 91% ─ 95% if we consider the most important clients in terms of spending.

Anyway, despite the good premises, just yesterday I started buying it. Why? A matter of price.

When I first analyzed FactSet it was the end of 2022, and the price was firmly $400 per share, with some peaks that almost reached $500. The P/E Ratio (TTM) never fell below 30x, a price multiple that I considered excessive. I intended to buy some shares once the $350 level was reached, but that would have meant a price drop of at least 25%, something that historically happened just a couple of times. Likelihoods were not on my side.

When a company has such a sound business model, it is much more resilient in facing adverse macroeconomic contexts, which is why FactSet never dropped much more than 20%. But then something suddenly happened: an old alert sounded at $300 per share. That meant a 40% drop from the all-time high, which in recent history occurred only during the peak of the GFC.

The trigger was a Q4 FY2025 that didn’t please the market, leading the stock to plunge by 10% despite the trend already being bearish. But what happened that was so serious?

Q4 FY2025 analysis

This slide summarizes the results of this quarter, and as you can see, the picture doesn’t look so negative to justify such a collapse. Organic revenues went up by 4.50%, adjusted net income rose by 7.30%, and adjusted diluted EPS grew by 8.30% thanks to the buyback.

The flaw that worried investors the most was the reduction of the adjusted operating margin by 200 bps, attributed mainly to increasing competition driven by new AI developments, or at least this is what the bearish thesis supports. In a sense, the market thinks that AI is not a growth catalyst for FactSet but a negative factor able to replace its platform.

Another signal that investors didn’t appreciate was the FY2026 guidance about adjusted diluted EPS, forecasted to grow in the 0-3.50% range. For a company like FactSet that managed to improve this data for 29 years in a row, breaking the record is viewed as the beginning of a new era (for the worst).

Definitely not positive news, however, I think we should give the proper weight to these statistics. The goal of an investor should be to understand where the company is in 10 years, not the next year. If a temporary stalemate is necessary to fuel the long-term prospects, then so be it.

The market narrative sees FactSet as an obsolete company and it will not be so distant the moment when AI replaces it. But is this really the case? In my opinion the reality couldn’t be further from the truth. Here is why.

Firstly, FactSet is not a company that can be easily replaced by any AI model. Over the decades it has proved to be one of the most reliable data providers in terms of quality and size. If you were a portfolio manager responsible for an AUM of $billions, would you rely on Chat GPT for your financial analysis/portfolio management? At best, you would use AI to just get food for thought, but you wouldn’t question the underlying platform that provides you with trustworthy data. From this standpoint, AI is not even close.

You should consider FactSet as a huge ecosystem within which every financial operator can basically do everything it needs. Getting reliable and live data, building custom valuation models, finding investment ideas and much more. People employed in this industry know really well how important FactSet is to make more reasonable investment decisions. At the end of the day, numbers speak for themselves.

A company that sells obsolete products doesn’t increase its clients/users every single year. By the way, the last one experienced a significant jump. And bear in mind that an annual subscription can cost tens of thousands of dollars, so a company would easily do without it if it weren't truly essential.

AI becomes a threat if the company remains anchored to a product that no longer meets today's requirements, but this is not the case at all. Sanoke Viswanathan has recently joined as CEO, but he is already determined to know what process the company needs to go through in order to integrate AI features into the platform:

What I meant by the work ahead is, this is an accelerating and really dynamic space. There is a lot of technology change. There is a lot of competitive dynamics as in new competitors, start-ups and traditional competitors, all in an AI arms race.

So my focus in the next several weeks and months is going to be spending significant time with our clients to understand their top priorities, how they are transforming their own workflows and spend tons of time with teams internally that our product evolution and looking at our road map and understanding how we are meeting our client demand.

To be precise, AI is not something new for FactSet, in fact, it has already been integrated into pre-existing features within the platform:

We really had a fantastic sales quarter and lots of momentum going into 2026. And AI was significant contributor to that. About 60% of what we have sold in terms of our AI tooling and content came in Q4. And I think that really bodes well for what is ahead of us and the opportunity that Sanoke spoke about. So in addition to discrete sales that we are referencing when we are talking about the numbers, our GenAI tooling and strategy has been pivotal in many of our renewals and new deals. So about 35% of our renewals in the quarter mentioned our GenAI tooling and strategy as a contributor to their decision to select FactSet in head-to-head competition versus others.

Goran Skoko; Executive VP, Chief Revenue Officer and MD of EMEA & Asia Pacific, Q4 earnings call.

These data describe exactly the opposite: FactSet is embracing new technologies in its ecosystem and customers are pleased. Looking at the situation from this perspective, AI is an opportunity rather than an imminent enemy.

Investors are concerned that, to keep up the AI revolution, FactSet investments might reduce profitability too much. It might be, but I think we should also see the other side of the coin.

Sure, Capex is increasing and profitability is worsening, but this bitter pill is the medicine for long-term prosperity. Today we are dealing with the AI revolution, 10-20 years ago it was something else. The technological progress never stopped, yet FactSet always ended up better than ever.

And the investments that we are making and what I see in the hopper are all constructive and logical and natural extensions of where we are today.

Sanoke Viswanathan, CEO & Director, Q4 call.

With this statement, the CEO managed to fully capture what I think: what is happening today is a natural process that will bring the FactSet platform to a higher level, the opposite of being replaced.

Finally, one last note about margins and 0-3.50% growth prospects of diluted EPS. On the one hand, this is attributable to new investments aimed at delivering a better service; on the other, it is the result of a rather conservative estimate.

We are taking a conservative approach to our guidance to reflect the current environment of longer sales cycles and more rigorous client approval processes, and not due to reduced confidence in our competitive positioning or market demand.

Helen Shan, Executive VP & CFO, Q4 call.

It was never a matter of demand or competitive advantage erosion, something pointed out by the bearish narrative. Anyway, since the approach was conservative, I wouldn’t be surprised if the FY2026 guidance gets an upward revision over the next quarters.

Conclusion

FactSet is in the midst of a process of transformation (hopefully for the better), something it has already faced several times throughout its history. I don’t believe its competitive advantage is at risk, and I do consider the $300 per share an interesting price to buy some shares. If the rating was a buy at $400, it is definitely a strong buy at $300.

Price multiples are very low compared to historical levels, and I keep believing this company can sustain a long-term growth rate of at least 8-10%. That’s why I think FactSet is currently one of the best options available in terms of risk-reward.

PS: While I am writing this article, I noticed that the stock price fell even lower than $300, so it is even cheaper now. I am planning to buy more shares if the price gets to $235. I don’t think it will, but who knows.

data.title: 5 Best Stocks As SEC Weighs Trump Earnings Report Proposal
data.description:
Trump’s Proposal to End Quarterly Reporting

On Sept. 15, President Donald Trump used Truth Social to urge the SEC to end quarterly earnings reports and move U.S. public companies to semi-annual reporting. He argued it would “save money” and let managers “focus on properly running their companies,” noting any change is subject to SEC approval.

While it’s uncertain if President Trump’s proposal will get the nod from the SEC, now can be a good time to prepare a strategy for selecting the best stocks to buy that are accustomed to a semiannual mandate. A smart place to begin the search is international equities, as many non-U.S. countries do not have quarterly reporting requirements. Below, I’ve highlighted some nations that do not require quarterly reporting and the potential impact if the Securities and Exchange Commission approves Trump’s proposal.

Countries and Regions That Don’t Require Quarterly Reporting

The demands of investors and analysts seeking more frequent updates to financials, in addition to market impacts and evolving policies, have prompted some international companies to include quarterly reporting. While many international markets do not mandate quarterly reporting for publicly traded companies, a number of countries and regions still offer it, including:

  • United Kingdom: The FCA removed the old interim management statement (IMS) requirement in November 2014; firms can still voluntarily issue Q1/Q3 “trading updates.”

  • EU/EEA member states: The Transparency Directive amendments abolished mandatory quarterly reporting for countries like France, Germany, Spain, Italy, the Netherlands, Sweden, Denmark, Ireland, and Norway.

  • Switzerland & South Africa: Half-year required; Quarterly reporting optional.

  • Singapore: Risk-based: Quarterly reporting only for higher-risk issuers since 2020.

  • Hong Kong: No compulsory quarterly reporting; GEM mandate removed in 2024.

  • Australia/New Zealand: Listed “disclosing entities” file half-year interim reports; quarterly reporting is not universal.

Potential Impact If The SEC Approves of Trump’s Proposal

If Trump’s semi-annual reporting requirement is adopted, investors will experience two formal earnings “seasons” instead of four, which could reduce near-term volatility. Sectors with long capex cycles (energy, metals and mining, defense, some infrastructure, and telecom) could benefit. Conversely, sectors like financials that include banks and insurers may suffer from less frequent transparency.

Seasonality, as we know it now, would likely shift: Q1 and Q3 “mini seasons” would shrink, and positioning may bunch into H1 and FY windows, potentially increasing gap risk around those dates. Empirically, regions that eased quarterly mandates, such as the EU, still see many firms update voluntarily, so the practical impact depends on how much U.S. companies choose to keep quarterly updates.

How Earnings Seasonality Could Shift if Semi-Annual

If quarterly reporting is no longer required, seasonality will be impacted, but many companies may still choose to continue the tradition as shareholders demand transparency. Given the macroeconomic backdrop, Seeking Alpha's top international stocks showcase strong fundamentals that can perform well in an environment of persistent inflation, geopolitical risks, and a weakening dollar. Each of the stocks has favorable Factor Grades that are collectively strong based on value, growth, profitability, momentum, and analysts' earnings revisions, earning them a Strong Buy Quant Rating. To narrow down our list of best stocks to buy if the SEC approves Trump’s semi-annual earnings report proposal, we focused on names that:

  • Are Quant-Rated “Strong Buy”.

  • Are headquartered in countries or geographic regions that don’t mandate quarterly reporting (or otherwise directly benefit from the U.S. rule change).

  • Operate in long-cycle sectors that are less dependent on quarterly check-ins, and

  • Have performed well in the current macro environment: persistent inflation, a softer USD, and geopolitical tension.

5 Best International Stocks to Buy That Already Report Earnings Semi-Annually

Many international companies report financials semi-annually, yet choose to report quarterly when listed on U.S. exchanges. There are benefits and drawbacks to both, as highlighted by analyst Julia Ostian. Three of my international selections (Gold Fields Limited, VEON Ltd., and Millicom International Cellular) offer quarterly reports. Heidelberg Materials and Iberdrola trade OTC in the U.S., primarily report in their home markets, and usually rely on the Rule 12g3-2(b) exemption rather than full SEC periodic filings.

1. Heidelberg Materials AG (OTCPK:HDLMY)

  • Market Capitalization: $42.23B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 14 out of 275.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 1 out of 11.

  • Sector: Materials.

  • Industry: Construction Materials.

One of the world’s largest cement and aggregates suppliers, Heidelberg Materials is tied to long-cycle infrastructure and construction demand. The company's vertical integration and decarbonization investments have helped support margins through inflationary periods. HDLMY was also once featured in the PRO Quant Portfolio, a data-driven strategy with weekly rebalancing and up to 30 stock ideas.

As a German (EU) issuer, HDLMY reports half-year and annual results with optional Q1/Q3 updates, aligning with our semi-annual screen. The cement/aggregates business is capex-intensive and driven by multi-year demand (infrastructure, rebuilding, defense-adjacent works), so fewer mandated earnings reports don’t contribute to short-term seasonality risks.

Company results for H1 2025 highlight seasonality and project execution, which is the sort of narrative investors will weigh more heavily if quarterly reporting goes away, and the macro mix of inflation resilience and potential USD weakness generally supports global materials pricing.

Seeking Alpha Factor Grades rate investment characteristics on a sector-relative basis. HDLMY's Profitability Grade is supported by strong cash generation and durable margins, so investors can focus more on multi-year fundamentals rather than needing four earnings checkpoints. HDMLY's Momentum indicates the market is already rewarding its improving outlook, as demonstrated by upward analysts' revisions. Heidelberg's grades suggest a long-cycle, pricing-power story where the signal (profitability + upward revisions) comfortably outweighs quarter-to-quarter noise, which is ideal for a semi-annual cadence. But if you’re not into construction materials and are looking to capitalize on the gold rush, consider my next stock.

2. Gold Fields Limited (GFI)

  • Market Capitalization: $33.53B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 5 out of 275.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 4 out of 47.

  • Sector: Materials.

  • Industry: Gold.

Gold Fields Limited is a South Africa–based gold producer with multi-asset operations across South Africa, Ghana, Australia, and Peru, focused on long-life, low-cost mines. Its cash flow and valuation are highly leveraged to bullion prices, making it a classic beneficiary of inflation fear and geopolitical risk.

GFI fits our screen on three counts: It operates in a semi-annual regime (South Africa’s JSE requires half-year and annual reports while quarterly reports are voluntary), it’s in a long-cycle, capex-heavy sector where fewer formal check-ins reduce unhelpful quarter-to-quarter noise, and it has clear macro leverage to persistent inflation, geopolitics, and a softer USD via gold prices.

As mentioned in our recent Top 5 Gold Mining Stocks article, GFI is sitting on 44.3 M oz of mineral reserves, offers outstanding momentum, and is up more than 175% in the past year. Record bullion prices and increased production lifted GFI H1 net profit by 162% YoY to $1.02B.

GFI Performance vs. Sector Peers

Recent H1 2025 results underscore the company’s focus on multi-year execution rather than quarterly optics, which is exactly the cadence this thesis favors.

GFI’s Valuation grade is at a C+, but PEG FWD of 0.37 is a 72% discount to the sector—PEG is one of my favorite valuation metrics. GFI has a high-quality, geographically diverse gold mining portfolio and another top Strong Buy gold stock with massive growth projections, along with outstanding Profitability, Momentum, and Revisions grades. While GFI’s international presence spans much of the southern hemisphere, my next stock engages in the global supply of energy on- and off-shore.

3. Iberdrola, S.A. (OTCPK:IBDRY)

  • Market Capitalization: $118.29B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 1 out of 110.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 1 out of 42.

  • Sector: Utilities.

  • Industry: Electric Utilities.

Spain’s Iberdrola is a global utility centered on regulated electricity networks and a large renewables pipeline (onshore/offshore wind, solar). Incredible scale, diversification, and rate-base growth have resulted in the company's inflation-resilient cash flows and less dependence on quarter-to-quarter price swings.

IBDRY’s Spain (EU) headquarters means semi-annual reporting, and its renewables model makes it a long-horizon, rate-base/capex story—not one that lives or dies by quarterly beats. Its A- Growth Grade is supported by solid EPS growth of 13.64% YoY, with Networks as a key driver. Iberdrola’s regulated grid business benefited from a larger asset base and the integration of Electricity North West (ENW) in the UK, lifting network results by ~31% and contributing over half of total EBITDA.

The company’s recent H1 2025 results and capital raise emphasize multi-year grid investment in the U.S. and UK, a backdrop where semi-annuals can sharpen focus on pipeline, allowed returns, and funding rather than quarter-to-quarter noise.

IBDRY Performance vs. Sector Peers

Macro-wise, regulated network economics, global scale, and utilities sector tailwinds help navigate inflation and geopolitical cross-currents, while a softer USD can aid ADR translation.

IBDRY is inherently long-cycle rather than quarter-to-quarter given its cash flows are anchored in regulated utilities. Steady margins from networks and renewables reinforce its A+ Profitability Grade. Investors can underwrite value on multi-year capex plans and dividend visibility without four earnings checkpoints. In addition, A+ Revisions shows analysts are raising estimates as new project milestones and tariff updates flow through, which is evidence that trading statements and regulatory filings can keep the market informed between H1/FY reports.

Put together, IBDRY’s sector profile and grade mix argue that a semi-annual cadence preserves the signal while trimming quarterly noise.

4. Millicom International Cellular S.A. (TIGO)

  • Market Capitalization: $8.03B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 10 out of 230.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 4 out of 19.

  • Sector: Communication Services.

  • Industry: Wireless Telecommunication Services.

Luxembourg-based Millicom International Cellular S.S. operates under the Tigo brand across Latin America, combining mobile, fiber/cable broadband, and fintech services. It’s an infrastructure-heavy, multi-year build-out story aimed at expanding ARPU and cash generation as penetration and monetization rise.

TIGO neatly matches all of our selection criteria. Its Luxembourg location sits in a framework where half-year plus full-year reporting is the legal minimum. Companies often add trading updates, but not full quarterly reports.

TIGO Performance vs. Sector Peers

The telecom infrastructure model is inherently long-cycle for TIGO, making it less dependent on quarterly earnings choreography, and the macro setup (potential USD softness, inflation pass-through via pricing, and LatAm demand) is constructive.

TIGO’s key Growth drivers were mobile postpaid strength and a recovering fixed-broadband trend. TIGO demonstrates strong free cash flow, has an incredible $1.68B in cash from operations, and $7.68 cash per share TTM vs. the sector median of $1.74. TIGO's A- Profitability grade signals improving margins and operating discipline, giving investors confidence to underwrite the multi-year cash flow ramp without four formal checkpoints. In addition to bullish momentum rewarding its execution (deleveraging) and reducing reliance on a steady stream, a semi-annual cadence complemented by trading updates makes this stock an attractive option for investors, as I dive into the final selection.

5. VEON Ltd. (VEON)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.65B.

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy.

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 3 out of 230.

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/19/2025): 1 out of 19.

  • Sector: Communication Services.

  • Industry: Wireless Telecommunication Services.

VEON is an emerging-markets telecom and digital-services group with key operations in Ukraine (Kyivstar), Pakistan (Jazz), and Bangladesh (Banglalink). Its multi-year network and fintech build-outs, plus potential EM currency tailwinds, make results better judged over half-year and full-year horizons.

Telecom networks are infrastructure-intensive and planned on multi-year capex cycles. EM data demand plus a weaker USD serve as potential tailwinds for VEON. The company also stands to benefit directly if the SEC moves the U.S. to semi-annuals because VEON files in the U.S., as fewer mandated 10-Qs would streamline disclosure for a capex-heavy operator.

VEON’s Q2 earnings call reported solid execution and solid results with revenue up 57% year-over-year, driven by direct digital revenues, expansion of mobile money platforms, and increased demand across emerging markets.

VEON Performance vs. Sector Peers

Note: VEON’s HQ moved to Dubai in 2024, and UAE exchanges require quarterly financials. However, the semi-annual favorability is still there, and execution momentum strengthens the long-horizon story that semi-annuals reward, so we give this one a pass on the reporting frequency criterion.

Bullish momentum and VEON’s A in Valuation suggest the shares are priced attractively relative to fundamentals, giving investors room to be patient through a longer H1/FY cadence without needing four beat/miss moments to justify the position. VEON exemplifies the broader message of this article: If the SEC pivots to semi-annual reporting, investors should favor long-cycle, capex-anchored businesses with supportive Quant grades, like our five selections.

Conclusion: 5 Best Stocks to Buy That Are Already in Line With Trump’s Earnings Report Proposal

Whether or not the SEC embraces a shift to semi-annual reporting, investors can prepare now. Fewer mandated updates would reduce short-term noise, elevate interim seasons, and favor businesses built on multi-year capex and backlogs. Our five selections, GFI, VEON, TIGO, HDLMY, and IBDRY, meet four tests: Strong Buy Quant Rating, semi-annual reporting jurisdictions (or direct U.S. filing benefit), sectors less reliant on quarterly reporting, and resilience to persistent inflation, a softer dollar, and rising geopolitical risk.

Still, ratings and macro conditions move fast. Confirm live Quant and Factor Grades, track guidance and trading statements, and size positions so gap risk remains tolerable for portfolios.

data.title: Oracle: A Great Way To Use An Option Collar (Technical Analysis And Options)
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) had the type of 1-day market cap move that holders dream, rallying more than 30% on September 10. That translated to a $240 billion gain in market capitalization, as its quarterly report highlighted a pile of new business contracts, related, of course, to artificial intelligence.

ORCL: high quality, far from cheap

And, well covered by Wall Street. Why anyone thinks they have a true edge on this type of stock nowadays is a mystery to me.

Looking at Seeking Alpha's factor grades, there is no mystery. Very overvalued, highly profitable, and awesome price momentum. But that last factor tends to be very backward-looking. ORCL was really off the radar for many, but the past few months have put it front and center again.

Technical analysis…my way

My articles here always include a bunch of charts. But since this one is more technically focused than my usual macro or ETF analysis, before I show some pictures to reflect my conclusions, I'll remind the audience of what my process is. Because I am neither a "traditional" market technician nor a Certified Market Technician (CMT), the chartist's version of the CFA for fundamental analysts, or the CFP for financial planning.

When I was moving up the ladder in this industry, technical analysis was considered just above voodoo in terms of skill set and acceptance. My late father was no voodoo artist, but he was a technician, through and through. He taught me when I was a teenager, and I am now blessed to be able to pass on that wisdom to those I mentor, coach and publish to. Dad was never a professional in this field, but he hoped I would be. I'm 30 years into it professionally, so check that box. And one of my three children is following in my footsteps to some degree, which is about the coolest thing ever to us.

How I "lost" on ORCL

I'll "take the L" as they say on ORCL in terms of the ratings on the only article I have written on them here. $175 to around $120 in under 6 months, good for a 30%+ decline on a Sell rating. Then it rallied by more than 180%!

I'll leave it to the audience to decide if I was "right or wrong" on the stock. Because, while I respect any investor who has a process they have learned and stick to it, I don't invest that way. My process is different, as noted above. And it works for me, as it did for those I managed money for in pre-retirement life.

We can't ignore how ORCL's path forward was essentially changed overnight with that last quarter's announcement. However, my process is about taking that as being "in the stock," as well as all other public information.

Charting to manage risk, once I know I want to own it

My chart approach is NOT about "picking winners" per se. My take is that ANY investment can go up at ANY time for ANY reason. But the difference between any 2 securities is the amount of RISK OF MAJOR DOWNSIDE that each includes at any point in time. THAT is what I chart. If this explains why my "ratings" here are both less emphasized to you and less important to me, that's why. Because in my portfolio, each position is just another risk asset (except for my T-bills and zero-coupon bond ladder).

For instance, while I do not currently own ORCL (but I do own S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 tracking ETFs, so essentially I do own it indirectly), if I did, there's a difference in risk that has nothing to do with what companies they are. If I own two companies that each occupy 5% of my total portfolio, that means that 10% of my total is at risk with them. If they both fall 10%, my entire portfolio drops 1%. If they both fall 40%, my entire portfolio falls 4%.

However, if I own 1% each in those 2 stocks, my dollars at risk are so much lower. Or more to the point, if I can own a higher percentage in them but strategically use put options or other option approaches to confront what I see in the charts, NOW I might be able to have my cake and eat it too!

That's why I'm incorporating some of that background on my personal style into this particular article. Because risk management is about setting one's "line in the sand" and using a range of tools to accomplish that. And adjusting tactically along the way.

Note that I am from this point forward going to focus on the charts. Not with lots of fancy terms like "RSI" and "MACD" or even "golden cross/death cross." That's all fun for CMTs. But I've looked at literally millions of charts since the 1980s (first on graph paper, then on a desktop computer, and now anywhere, any time).

This is my personal "brand" of technical analysis, which hopefully helps readers understand what I see, why I see it, and why it has been my bread and butter for decades, just with continuous upgrading in the ability to perform this analysis. Thanks to technology.

Charting ORCL: a threat to roll over

With ORCL, I'll use both daily and weekly charts to gain different perspectives.

While ORCL's one-day move up was, in percentage terms, a historic move, that is exactly why I take price trend analysis so seriously, no matter how much of Wall Street dismisses it. A 36% move in a day is great if you held the stock a day before and had not sold it. However, for some investors, that could be the entire run they were expecting.

So like the dog catching the car, it finally has what it wants. But does it know what it wants to do with it?

Charting is not an exact science. And neither are factors, valuation metrics, narratives or any other aspect of investment selection. However, ORCL is a great example of how investing is not about "what I own" as much as it is "what do I do with what I own?" And, the aforementioned "How do I want to own it?"

This daily chart is in a very dangerous spot. Yet it is also exhibiting a pattern that often represents the so-called "pause that refreshes." Some technical systems look for exactly this type of surge in price and pullback to start getting interested. Almost as if to say "Let the others eat first, and I'll fill my plate after they clear out."

I am not a fan of that approach, preferring to identify stocks and ETFs that are more gradual in their transition from a down cycle to a rising one. And that PPO is just starting to hint that ORCL's price is getting "tired." Like I said, I'm going to minimize the fancy lingo here and use analogies to real life whenever possible.

The other concern I have around stocks like this is the sheer numbers involved. In round figures, it went from $220 to $340 to $290. All in 2 weeks' time! Say what you want about earnings projections, P/E ratios and the like. I say we are in a time when a lot of that has not to be forgotten but minimized. Blame the algorithmic trading that now consumes daily volume, not me.

This one moved so violently in both directions, up 40% and then down 15% quickly. This is more about risk management around the position than straight price analysis. If you're a defense-first type like me, you reduced or hedged your position before the close on that magical up day. If you are a "long-term bull" on ORCL then…well, I don't know, I'm not one.

Here's the weekly chart of ORCL. I don't think I've ever seen this in all the years I've been charting, but see that big 1-day spike? I almost confused it for a trend line I drew to mark the chart! Still, you can see that same type of acceleration in the move higher, from late 2022 through very recently, and then the 2-headed monster of a move during this current quarter.

Note how "cleanly" the lines can be drawn. I know full well that in this community, price trend analysis is far less acknowledged compared to fundamentals. But if there's any reason for a charting skeptic to take it more seriously, it is this: algorithmic traders, big hedge funds and quant shops all rely VERY heavily on the type of analysis I'm presenting.

I not only know this from reading about those market players for years. I've seen it develop over decades, the now-rapid adoption of these methods. Because there was a time when folks who invested like I do were sparse. That helped make the observations like these on ORCL that much more effective.

But now? With smart young programmers and even AI-driven investing groups watching more closely, there is a much greater "self-fulfilling prophesy" about charting stocks and ETFs. That's one reason a stock like ORCL has been so "well-behaved" in recent years, staying within that price range, albeit rising steadily throughout. The PPO is stretched as you can see, but it also just re-accelerated.

Finally, I'll outline the approach I would take to this stock if I were out of it (I am) but was told I must "get exposure" in some manner right now. That puts me in the same boat with anyone who owns it or has decided they want to own this iconic tech leader.

I'll assume that in both cases, the mandate is to be "long" the stock. That is, be in a position to get some upside if they continue to rise. But the risk management aspect is optional. Literally, but also figuratively!

First, I'll outline 4 different ways to approach Oracle stock, then pick from that short list of routes.

4 ways I "get exposure" to a stock or ETF

Again, I'm more focused on HOW MUCH exposure I want to have to any stock at any time and HOW I want to get access to it. My typical methods include any of the following, and often more than one of them at a time:

1. Own shares of the stock (for a trade, investment or both).

2. Own call options on the stock (choosing expiration date and strike price based on what the chart tells me).

3. Collar the stock with it with options (i.e., for every 100 shares of stock buy 1 put option to protect it and sell 1 covered call option to help pay for the puts, struck at a price level I can live with if forced to sell).

4. Buy puts to try to profit from a price decline. This is the only straight "bear" strategy among the 3.

Getting exposure to ORCL right now

1. The stock is way overpriced technically. But that only means the risk of loss is high. That NEVER means there can't be more upside. Here in September of 2025, similar to the dot-com bubble, the global financial crisis and other pivotal eras in stock market history, I consider this to be a case of high risk AND high reward. So my chosen route is to own the stock. But as they say, not without a net!

That would normally lead me to a collar, but there's a potential hurdle. To simulate my own decision-making process, I'm writing this before looking at the option collar tables. However, as someone who does this regularly and shares my steps and trades with our Sungarden Investing Club members, I know that market volatility as represented by the S&P VIX Index (VIX) has been so low that options are cheap to buy. But that also means it is expensive to sell.

In other words, insurance on the downside (buying puts) costs relatively less than usual. So does taking a shot at higher upside (buying calls). But selling options, such as covered calls (like those popular ETFs do) still brings in cash flow. However, at a much lower rate than it has been in general in the past several years. That all goes back to the level of volatility exhibited by the S&P 500.

Ah, but these stocks are not the S&P 500, even if they are part of it. And they have been so volatile, in both directions (recall ORCL just this month), that could actually make them good collar candidates.

A collar is most applicable for me when volatility is high. I'll pay up for that put protection if the calls offset much of the cost of that protection. When a stock is volatile, that's more likely.

I'd say that for every 10 stocks I look at collars on these days, maybe 1 or at most 2 have even a prayer of being good candidates. ORCL is one of those. I get a hint of that just by looking at the "IV Rank" below. At 70%, it means that in the past 12 months, ORCL's volatility is currently in the highest 30% of that time's range. Very high. That's good for collars, not as good for buying an option without funding it in part or in full.

The table above shows that as of Thursday's close, ORCL traded around $297 a share. I could pay $25.60 a share on every 100-share trade lot to be able to sell the stock at $270 any time between now and 3/20/26, the option expiration date. So around 9-10% downside risk. You can see other combinations of $270 puts with call options struck at different prices.

The $350 calls to that same date (3/20/25) mean ORCL can rise to $350 before I'd be at risk of losing the stock. Getting it "called" away from me. That typically doesn't happen until at or close to the expiration date, and let's face it, that's a high-class problem. And if you believe, like I do, that markets have a memory, I'll note that the peak price on that huge recent move was north of $340 a share. Those calls bring in $24.40 a share, or $2,440 per 100 shares/1 covered call option.

THAT is the big attraction, described in more granular terms, of covered call ETFs. Being paid $2,400 for the obligation to sell 100 ORCL if it hits $350 in the next 6 months? An upcoming article will drill down on that subject again. Many love the income, and I do too. But if a stock declines and doesn't get back up fairly soon, it can open a can of worms. Many are good with that, as long as some income keeps flowing. Not me, at least in most cases.

Sticking to the matter at hand, the cost of the puts is nearly offset by the cash inflow from the calls. That's fairly rare these days, out 6 months like this one is. That's why the cost column above shows 0.4%, practically a "costless collar" on ORCL, with nearly a 2:1 ratio of upside to downside. I'm not buying ORCL or options on it, but like I said, if the mandate was to do "something," I'd take at least a moderate position for that tradeoff.

A starting point to manage risk in these odd markets

Hopefully this article serves to provide a timely example to get you in the mindset so you can start on your own path. I know this, my financial life, and therefore my happiness, owes a lot to what my late father taught me about charting and what I taught myself, in a way that made sense to me, about combining that price trend work with option strategies to manage risk.

data.title: Reddit: Market Is Ignoring Stagnating User Growth - Downgrade To Sell
data.description:
Reddit (NYSE:RDDT) appears to have a knack for proving the doubters wrong. The stock had come to the public markets with a large degree of skepticism but showed that it can deliver aggressive top- and bottom-line growth “at will.” The stock had stumbled last quarter on fears of impact from changes in the Google Search algorithm (GOOGL) but then proceeded to deliver incredible growth rates. That said, the stock valuation has run far too ahead of itself. I note that investors appear to be overlooking the stagnating user growth, which appears to already be hurt by tough comparables. I expect the stock to re-rate lower as it becomes clear that growth rates must moderate. I am downgrading the stock to a "Sell" rating.

RDDT Stock Price

I last covered RDDT in June, where I downgraded the stock on concerns of tough comparables ahead. The stock has soared 90% since.

While I clearly did not expect the strong second quarter results, I view much of the rally as being symptomatic of an aggressive and overheated broader market.

RDDT Stock Key Metrics

RDDT is a social media company that I like to think of as being “the one forum to end all forums.” RDDT uses the “thread” format that is widely known today and applies it across any kind of topic that you can think of. One can also think of RDDT as being a more organized Facebook (META) Groups.

In the most recent quarter, RDDT delivered 78% YoY revenue growth to $499.6 million, crushing guidance of $410 million to $430 million.

The company delivered this in large part by increasing average revenue per user, which jumped 47%. The rest was driven by a solid 21% YoY growth rate in daily active users. I note that U.S. users were curiously stagnant QoQ, but international users continued the solid growth, perhaps driven by the company’s use of generative AI for machine translation. I view this application of AI, rather than the data licensing revenues, as the reason driving the market’s perception of the stock as being a generative AI winner.

RDDT also delivered solid gains in profitability, generating an impressive 18% GAAP net margin and 33% adjusted EBITDA margin.

RDDT ended the quarter with $2 billion of cash versus no debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided for the third quarter to see up to $545 million in revenue, representing 56.4% YoY growth.

On the conference call, management noted that the platform is “the No. 1 most cited domain for AI across all models per data collected by Profound.” Management noted that the Google Search algorithm changes that emerged in the first quarter are still impacting, but that “in July, we added DAUQs in both the U.S. and internationally.” The skeptic in me would point out that stagnating user growth, especially in the most lucrative US market, is the biggest risk for social networks. At some point the company might face some issues in showing more ads or charging more per ad, and I note that the company might start to see tough comparables to user count as soon as the first quarter of next year.

Is RDDT Stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, RDDT traded hands at an astonishing 26x sales. That represents over a 150% premium over Meta Platforms (META) and an even higher premium over names like Pinterest (PINS) and Snapchat (SNAP). Consensus estimates look aggressive, calling for the company to see several years of 20% to 30% growth ahead.

I expect those targets to prove too optimistic. The company is long overdue to face headwinds from tough comparables. I wouldn't be surprised if these headwinds present themselves as early as the fourth quarter, when the company begins lapping its $400 million quarterly revenue run rate. I again point to the stagnating user growth, which while still showing YOY growth, may make itself felt in revenue growth rates when the company begins lapping the tough comparables.

In addition to tough comparables, the company may also face risks from newly implemented tariffs as well as an overall tough macro environment. I have seen this all before - in the 2021 tech stock bubble, many tech winners had posted incredible top-line growth rates that suddenly vanished rapidly. In the case of RDDT, I believe that much of its recent growth comes from capturing the “low-hanging fruit” of an under-monetized platform, and that indeed was my thesis when I was bullish after the stock stumbled after its IPO. It is no longer clear that the platform is under-monetized. The company’s $7.87 US ARPU is now higher than the $7.29 at Pinterest (PINS) and within striking distance of the $8.33 at Snapchat (SNAP), in stark contrast to when it had its IPO. The stagnating user growth, especially in the crucial US market, is arguably the most important warning sign in the story today. I expect the company’s days of accelerated growth relative to peers to be reaching maturity and thus expect the valuation premium to compress significantly, down to around 12x sales, which would put it still at a premium to META and a sizable premium to PINS and SNAP. That represents 40% potential downside over the next 12 months, assuming the company can sustain 34.7% growth in 2026, and I caution that consensus estimates do not look conservative for next year.

RDDT Stock Conclusion

RDDT is delivering among the most aggressive top- and bottom-line growth among tech peers. The stock’s blistering run is emblematic of an overheated market, with the stock now trading at over a 150% premium to META despite a less-proven track record. Investors appear to be overlooking the stagnating user trends, which historically are a clear-cut sign of moderating growth rates. I recommend investors instead look towards META in the space, which is a core holding of the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. Given the mismatch between optimistic consensus estimates and aggressive valuations, I am downgrading the stock to a sell rating.

data.title: Polestar: It's Worse Than Thought
data.description:
When it comes to the electric vehicle, or EV, space, most companies have not lived up to the hype. A number of years ago, many of these names had lofty delivery and revenue projections that turned out to be way too optimistic, leading to dramatic falls in their share prices. This has certainly been the case with Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (NASDAQ:PSNY), which reported its first half of 2025 results earlier this month. Unfortunately, things are getting even worse here.

Previous coverage of the name

It was back in late August when I most recently took a look at Polestar. At that time, I cautioned investors against jumping in during the recent rally, as the situation remained rather bleak. While vehicle deliveries had grown nicely in the first half of the year, that was partially due to a really bad 2024, and the company's financial situation was still quite terrible. Since that time, shares have lost more than 15%, compared to a 2% plus rally in the S&P 500.

First half results and declining estimates

As a reminder, Polestar reported retail sales volumes up 51.1% for the first half of 2025. Total revenues ended up growing by 56.5% to over $1.42 billion. Of that increase, about $72 million was due to regulatory credit sales, some of which may be in jeopardy in the U.S. moving forward due to policy changes. The problem for Polestar is that its continuous takedowns of guidance over the years have sent revenue estimates much lower, as detailed in the table below.

As a reminder, when Polestar was trading as a SPAC back in 2022, the original goal was for nearly 300,000 deliveries this year, of which over 100,000 alone were to come from the Polestar 2. As struggles built up in 2023, the company brought that forecast down to about 155,000 to 165,000 vehicles for 2025, at which point it was hoping to be cash flow positive by the end of this year. The current plan is for 30% to 35% growth through 2027, which would imply about 110,000 deliveries by the end of that year.

The company reported a large gross loss due to a $724 million impairment expense. However, the adjusted gross margin of 1.4% for the first six months of the year was a reversal from the negative 2.6% seen a year earlier, mainly due to the increase in credit sales. The net loss for the first half would have been a nice improvement after taking out the impairment charge but it is still too large to keep this business going. Adjusted EBITDA was a negative $302 million, but that was a 30% improvement over the first two quarters of 2024.

For the second quarter, however, things were not as rosy. Revenue growth of 36.6% was about a percentage point and a half below the delivery increase. The adjusted gross margin of negative 5.7% was much worse than the positive 0.4% seen in the prior year's restated quarter. Even when taking out the impairment expense, the net loss would have been significantly higher than seen in Q2 2024. Polestar's cost of goods sold is starting to be negatively impacted by global trade wars, with management discussing the issue of tariffs on the most recent conference call, although they didn't provide specific numbers to the extent of the headwind.

The company currently sells three vehicles, all of which were designed to compete with the Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) Model 3 and Y. The Polestar 2, 3, and 4 all start in the $50,000 to $60,000 range, depending on configuration and current sales offers, although the 2 was in the low to mid $40,000 range for a time. This makes them a bit more expensive than their Tesla counterparts at the moment. The Polestar 5 is a luxury vehicle launching later this year that's expected to cost well over $100,000, with the Polestar 7, a premium compact SUV, set to launch in about three years.

Balance sheet weakens further

With such low delivery volumes over time, Polestar's large losses have led to significant cash burn. The company has mostly relied on debt to support its operations, but it did raise $200 million via equity earlier this year. The company doesn't always give us all of its financial information, but we do get at least two balance sheet updates a year. The chart below shows how working capital, which is current assets minus current liabilities, has gotten a lot worse over time.

While the company had more than $718 million of cash on the balance sheet at the end of June, it also had over $4.67 billion of total interest-bearing liabilities. Polestar is likely going to need to raise more money through equity, and that keeps getting tougher as the market cap continues to decline. At the end of 2022, for example, the company was worth about $14 billion, but today that sits at just $2.3 billion. There also isn't a timeline for when positive cash flows will finally start to regularly occur, as volumes need to be a lot higher.

A look at current valuations

As the chart below shows, Polestar trades at a price-to-sales ratio, based on current 2026 estimates, that's near the bottom end of its space, below even most of the Chinese EV companies such as NIO Inc. (NIO), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), and XPeng Inc. (XPEV). The only name currently cheaper is ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited (ZK). Polestar goes for just a fraction of the valuation of names like Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS) and Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID). EV giant Tesla trades at a low double-digit multiple on its price to expected sales, the main outlier in this group, so I didn't put it in the chart because it skews things quite a bit.

When it comes to street analysts, the picture is mixed right now. As of Friday morning, there was one sell rating against two holds. The average price target of $1.03 implied slight upside from the day's finish. That average valuation figure has been slashed from the mid $2s just a year ago, and it was in the low-double digits in late 2022 after Polestar completed its SPAC merger.

Final thoughts and recommendation

As it turns out, the situation at Polestar was even worse than I previously thought. Despite the surge in first half deliveries this year, large losses and cash burn are still ongoing, putting the name in a precarious situation. The potential loss of regulatory credit sales in the U.S. moving forward is another headwind, and the company will likely need a major equity infusion to help it manage a debt-heavy balance sheet.

Even with Polestar shares pulling back quite a bit since I last covered them, I have to keep my sell rating on the stock today. Delivery growth is not leading to great overall results yet, and the balance sheet remains in terrible shape. Should shares fall below a dollar here again, a reverse split may be needed, and that itself could be a negative catalyst. There's a lot of improvement that's needed here before I can even think about an upgrade.
